annotations.label: Quadratic form

anchor.snippet: a quadratic form is a polynomial with terms all of degree two

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `QuadraticForm R M := QuadraticMap R M R`, which generalizes the polynomial definition to any commutative semiring and module.

annotations.label: Definite vs isotropic (real)

anchor.snippet: a quadratic form is said to be definite if it takes the value zero only when all its variables are simultaneously zero

annotations.note: `QuadraticMap.Anisotropic Q := ∀ x, Q x = 0 → x = 0` is exactly the property described (an anisotropic / 'definite-in-this-sense' form).

annotations.label: Unary, binary, ternary forms

anchor.snippet: In the cases of one, two, and three variables they are called unary , binary , and ternary

annotations.note: This is purely terminological vocabulary about the number of variables and has no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Quadric from a quadratic form

anchor.snippet: a non-zero quadratic form in n variables defines an ( n − 2) -dimensional quadric

annotations.note: No `Quadric` type or projective vanishing-locus construction tied to quadratic forms is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Square of Euclidean norm

anchor.snippet: the square of the Euclidean norm expressing the distance between a point with coordinates

annotations.note: The squared Euclidean norm is computable via `EuclideanSpace.norm_eq` (norm = sqrt of sum of squared coordinates), but it is not explicitly identified as a `QuadraticForm` instance.

annotations.label: Quadratic space (preview)

anchor.snippet: A closely related notion with geometric overtones is a quadratic space

annotations.note: `QuadraticModuleCat` bundles a module with a `QuadraticForm`; Mathlib treats 'quadratic space' as the pair (M, Q : QuadraticForm R M) rather than a stand-alone abbrev.

annotations.label: Fermat's theorem on sums of two squares

anchor.snippet: Fermat's theorem on sums of two squares , which determines when an integer may be expressed in the form

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime.sq_add_sq` gives the prime case (`p % 4 ≠ 3 ⇒ ∃ a b, p = a^2 + b^2`); `Nat.eq_sq_add_sq_iff` extends to all integers.

annotations.label: Pell's equation

anchor.snippet: He considered what is now called Pell's equation , x 2 − ny 2 = 1

annotations.note: `Pell.IsPell` characterizes elements b of `ℤ√d` with `b * star b = 1`, which encodes the Pell equation x² − dy² = 1 but is not stated as a direct equation predicate.

annotations.label: Quadratic form from a matrix

anchor.snippet: Any n × n matrix A determines a quadratic form q A in n variables

annotations.note: `Matrix.toQuadraticForm' M : QuadraticForm R (n → R)` sends `x ↦ row x * M * col x`, exactly the matrix-defined quadratic form.

annotations.label: Three-variable matrix form

anchor.snippet: Consider the case of quadratic forms in three variables

annotations.note: This is a worked example in three variables and is not formalized as a distinct theorem.

annotations.label: Same form iff matching diagonal and symmetric sums

anchor.snippet: two different matrices define the same quadratic form if and only if they have the same elements on the diagonal

annotations.note: No declaration explicitly characterizes equality of `Matrix.toQuadraticForm'` in terms of diagonal entries and symmetric off-diagonal sums.

annotations.label: Unique symmetric matrix for a quadratic form

anchor.snippet: is the unique symmetric matrix that defines q A

annotations.note: `QuadraticForm.toMatrix'` gives the symmetric matrix (with `isSymm_toMatrix'`) recovering Q via `associated`, but uniqueness as a stand-alone statement is not isolated.

annotations.label: Bijection between quadratic forms and symmetric matrices

anchor.snippet: there is a one-to-one correspondence between quadratic forms and symmetric matrices

annotations.note: `associated_left_inverse` and `toQuadraticMap_associated` together establish the bijection between quadratic forms and symmetric bilinear forms (assuming `Invertible (2 : R)`), not stated as a single equiv with matrices.

annotations.label: Jacobi's orthogonal diagonalization

anchor.snippet: Jacobi proved that, for every real quadratic form, there is an orthogonal diagonalization

annotations.note: `QuadraticForm.equivalent_weightedSumSquares` shows every quadratic form over a field with `Invertible (2:K)` is equivalent to a weighted sum of squares, which generalizes Jacobi's orthogonal diagonalization.

annotations.label: Sylvester's law of inertia

anchor.snippet: Sylvester's law of inertia states that the numbers of each 0 , +1 , and −1 are invariants of the quadratic form

annotations.note: `sigPos_of_equiv_weightedSumSquares` + `sigNeg_of_equiv_weightedSumSquares` (uniqueness) together with `equivalent_one_zero_neg_one_weighted_sum_squared` (existence) state Sylvester's law of inertia.

annotations.label: Signature of a quadratic form

anchor.snippet: The signature of the quadratic form is the triple

annotations.note: `sigPos` and `sigNeg` define the positive/negative parts of the signature; there is no bundled triple `(n₊, n₋, n₀)` invariant declaration.

annotations.label: Positive/negative definite, nondegenerate, isotropic

anchor.snippet: in this case the quadratic form is called positive definite (all +1 ) or negative definite (all −1 )

annotations.note: `QuadraticMap.PosDef`, `QuadraticMap.Anisotropic`, and `QuadraticMap.Nondegenerate` exist; negative-definite is expressed as `PosDef (-Q)` rather than as a distinct definition.

annotations.label: Discriminant of a quadratic form

anchor.snippet: The discriminant of a quadratic form , concretely the class of the determinant of a representing matrix

annotations.note: `QuadraticForm.discr b Q := (Q.toMatrix b).det` is the discriminant defined as the determinant of the matrix representation in a basis.

annotations.label: Change of basis for matrix of a quadratic form

anchor.snippet: the symmetric square matrix A is transformed into another symmetric square matrix B of the same size

annotations.note: `toMatrix_comp` (and `toMatrix'_comp`) shows the matrix of Q ∘ f equals fᵀ * (Q.toMatrix) * f, the standard change-of-basis transformation.

annotations.label: Jacobi's theorem (orthogonal diagonalization of symmetric matrix)

anchor.snippet: Any symmetric matrix A can be transformed into a diagonal matrix

annotations.note: `exists_orthogonal_basis` produces an orthogonal basis for any symmetric bilinear form over a field with `Invertible 2`, generalizing diagonalization of symmetric matrices.

annotations.label: Positive/negative indices of inertia

anchor.snippet: the numbers n + and n − are called the positive and negative indices of inertia

annotations.note: `sigPos` and `sigNeg` are the positive and negative indices of inertia, defined as the maximal finrank of a positive- (resp. negative-)definite subspace.

annotations.label: Positive definite, negative definite, isotropic (via values)

anchor.snippet: The quadratic form q is positive definite if

annotations.note: `QuadraticMap.PosDef Q := ∀ x, x ≠ 0 → 0 < Q x` exactly captures positive-definiteness via values; negative-definite is `PosDef (-Q)`.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of positive definite real form up to isometry

anchor.snippet: any positive definite quadratic form in n variables can be brought to the sum of n squares

annotations.note: `equivalent_one_neg_one_weighted_sum_squared` shows nondegenerate real forms are equivalent to a ±1-weighted sum of squares; the pure positive-definite-→-sum-of-squares specialization is not isolated.

annotations.label: Quadratic form over a field (coordinate-free)

anchor.snippet: A quadratic form over a field K is a map q : V → K from a finite-dimensional

annotations.note: `QuadraticForm R M` is the coordinate-free map `M → R` definition (over any commutative semiring), encompassing the field case.

annotations.label: n-ary quadratic form (polynomial)

anchor.snippet: an n -ary quadratic form over a field K is a homogeneous polynomial of degree 2

annotations.note: Mathlib's `QuadraticForm`/`QuadraticMap` is map-based; it is not defined as `MvPolynomial.IsHomogeneous n=2`, and no equivalence between the polynomial and map definitions is registered.

annotations.label: Null vector

anchor.snippet: is a null vector if

annotations.note: The predicate `Q v = 0` appears via `Anisotropic` ('no nonzero null vector') but there is no standalone `IsNullVector`-style definition.

annotations.label: Equivalent quadratic forms

anchor.snippet: Two n -ary quadratic forms φ and ψ over K are equivalent if there exists a nonsingular linear transformation

annotations.note: `QuadraticMap.Equivalent Q₁ Q₂ := Nonempty (Q₁.IsometryEquiv Q₂)` is the existence of a linear equivalence intertwining the two forms.

annotations.label: Symmetrization of coefficient matrix

anchor.snippet: The coefficient matrix A of q may be replaced by the symmetric matrix

annotations.note: `Matrix.toQuadraticForm'` plus `isSymm_toMatrix'` express that one can pass to a symmetric representing matrix; the explicit `(A+Aᵀ)/2` formula is not packaged as a lemma.

annotations.label: Associated bilinear form

anchor.snippet: The associated bilinear form of a quadratic form q is defined by

annotations.note: `QuadraticMap.associated` (via `associatedHom`) sends Q to the symmetric bilinear form `B(x,y) = (1/2)(Q(x+y) − Q(x) − Q(y))` when 2 is invertible.

annotations.label: Equivalence of symmetric bilinear forms and quadratic forms

anchor.snippet: the theories of symmetric bilinear forms and of quadratic forms in n variables are essentially the same

annotations.note: `associated_left_inverse` and `toQuadraticMap_associated` give the two-sided inverse between symmetric bilinear maps and quadratic maps when 2 is invertible.

annotations.label: Quadratic form on a vector space

anchor.snippet: a quadratic form on V is a function Q : V → K that has the following property

annotations.note: The `QuadraticMap` (and abbreviated `QuadraticForm`) structure axiomatizes precisely `Q(a • x) = a² • Q x` together with a companion bilinear map.

annotations.label: Quadratic space

anchor.snippet: A finite-dimensional vector space with a quadratic form is called a quadratic space

annotations.note: `QuadraticModuleCat` bundles a module with a quadratic form, the closest thing to a 'quadratic space' object; no standalone abbreviation requiring finite-dimensionality exists.

annotations.label: Q is homogeneous of degree 2

anchor.snippet: The map Q is a homogeneous function of degree 2

annotations.note: `QuadraticMap.map_smul : Q (a • x) = (a * a) • Q x` is the homogeneity-of-degree-2 axiom built into the structure.

annotations.label: Polar bilinear form (char ≠ 2)

anchor.snippet: When the characteristic of K is not 2, the bilinear map

annotations.note: `QuadraticMap.associated`, which requires `Invertible (2 : R)`, is exactly the polar bilinear form `B(x,y) = (1/2)(Q(x+y) − Q(x) − Q(y))`.

annotations.label: Characteristic 2 polar form is alternating

anchor.snippet: When the characteristic of K is 2, so that 2 is not a unit

annotations.note: `polar_self : polar Q x x = 2 • Q x` becomes 0 in characteristic 2, implying the alternating property; the char-2 specialization itself is not stated as a separate lemma.

annotations.label: Existence of (non-symmetric) bilinear B″ with B″(x,x)=Q(x) in char 2

anchor.snippet: there always exists a bilinear form B ″ (not in general either unique or symmetric) such that

annotations.note: `exists_companion : ∃ B : BilinMap R M N, ∀ x y, Q (x + y) = Q x + Q y + B x y` is precisely the existence of a (not necessarily symmetric) companion bilinear map.

annotations.label: Isometric quadratic spaces

anchor.snippet: Two n -dimensional quadratic spaces ( V , Q ) and ( V ′, Q ′) are isometric if there exists an invertible linear transformation

annotations.note: `QuadraticMap.IsometryEquiv Q₁ Q₂` is a linear equivalence with `Q₂ (f m) = Q₁ m`, exactly the notion of isometric quadratic spaces.

annotations.label: Isometry classes correspond to equivalence classes

anchor.snippet: The isometry classes of n -dimensional quadratic spaces over K correspond to the equivalence classes

annotations.note: `Equivalent` is defined as `Nonempty (Q₁.IsometryEquiv Q₂)`, so isometry classes and equivalence classes literally coincide by definition.

annotations.label: Associated quadratic form and polar form (over a ring)

anchor.snippet: A mapping q : M → R : v ↦ b ( v , v ) is the associated quadratic form of b

annotations.note: `BilinMap.toQuadraticMap B := fun x ↦ B x x` is the associated quadratic map of a bilinear form, paired with `QuadraticMap.polarBilin` in the other direction.

annotations.label: Equivalent characterizations of quadratic form over a ring

anchor.snippet: A quadratic form q : M → R may be characterized in the following equivalent ways

annotations.note: Mathlib's `QuadraticMap` adopts the `map_smul + exists_companion` characterization; equivalences with other ring-level characterizations of quadratic forms are not all packaged into lemmas.

annotations.label: Orthogonal vectors and kernel

anchor.snippet: Two elements v and w of V are called orthogonal if

annotations.note: `QuadraticMap.IsOrtho Q x y := Q (x + y) = Q x + Q y` defines orthogonality with respect to Q; the radical/kernel notion is `QuadraticMap.radical`.

annotations.label: Non-singular, isotropic, definite, totally singular

anchor.snippet: Q is non-singular if the kernel of its associated bilinear form is

annotations.note: `Nondegenerate`, `Anisotropic`, and `PosDef` cover non-singular/isotropic/definite, but 'totally singular' (Q vanishing on a subspace) is not given a dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Orthogonal group of a quadratic form

anchor.snippet: The orthogonal group of a non-singular quadratic form Q is the group of the linear automorphisms

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Matrix.orthogonalGroup` for the standard quadratic form on `n → R`, but no general group `O(Q)` of isometries `QuadraticMap.IsometryEquiv Q Q` is bundled.

annotations.label: Composition algebra

anchor.snippet: If a quadratic space ( A , Q ) has a product so that A is an algebra over a field

annotations.note: No `CompositionAlgebra` definition (algebra with a multiplicative quadratic form) is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Diagonalization of quadratic forms (char ≠ 2)

anchor.snippet: Every quadratic form q in n variables over a field of characteristic not equal to 2 is equivalent to a diagonal form

annotations.note: `equivalent_weightedSumSquares` exhibits every QuadraticForm over a field with `Invertible 2` as equivalent to a weighted sum of squares (a diagonal form).

annotations.label: Geometric nature via eigenvalues (ellipsoid/hyperboloid)

anchor.snippet: If all eigenvalues of A are non-zero, then the solution set is an ellipsoid or a hyperboloid

annotations.note: Mathlib does not classify level sets of quadratic forms as ellipsoids or hyperboloids based on eigenvalue signs.

annotations.label: Geometric nature with zero eigenvalues (paraboloid)

anchor.snippet: If there exist one or more eigenvalues

annotations.note: There is no Mathlib classification of degenerate quadratic level sets as paraboloids or cylinders.

annotations.label: Integral quadratic form

anchor.snippet: Quadratic forms over the ring of integers are called integral quadratic forms

annotations.note: `QuadraticForm ℤ M` is well-typed (since `QuadraticMap` works over any `CommSemiring`), but no dedicated `IntegralQuadraticForm` abbrev or theory is registered.

annotations.label: Integral with respect to a lattice

anchor.snippet: a quadratic form Q is integral with respect to Λ if and only if it is integer-valued on Λ

annotations.note: No definition of a real quadratic form being integral with respect to a lattice (taking integer values on lattice points) is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Universal integral quadratic form

anchor.snippet: An integral quadratic form whose image consists of all the positive integers is sometimes called universal

annotations.note: No notion of 'universal' integral quadratic form (image = all positive integers) appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lagrange's four-square theorem

anchor.snippet: Lagrange's four-square theorem shows that

annotations.note: `Nat.sum_four_squares : ∀ n, ∃ a b c d, a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + d^2 = n` is Lagrange's four-square theorem.

annotations.label: Ramanujan's 54 quaternary forms

anchor.snippet: Ramanujan generalized this

annotations.note: Ramanujan's classification of 54 universal diagonal quaternary forms is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: {1,2,5,5} missing 15

anchor.snippet: For example, {1, 2, 5, 5} has 15 as the exception

annotations.note: This specific counterexample about the form {1,2,5,5} not representing 15 is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 15 and 290 theorems

anchor.snippet: the 15 and 290 theorems have completely characterized universal integral quadratic forms

annotations.note: The Conway–Schneeberger 15 theorem and the Bhargava–Hanke 290 theorem are not present in Mathlib.
